one_line_takeaway: Defines the Zero-Shot Open-Set Recognition task and proposes ASE, which adversarially learns unknown-class semantic embeddings to train an unknowns-informed open-set classifier that reliably rejects unknown-class images while preserving unseen-class accuracy — a computer-vision recognition advance with no security threat model.
key_finding: Simply combining SOTA ZSL and OSR methods works poorly for ZS-OSR (open scores of unseen and unknown samples heavily overlap, Fig. 2). ASE substantially improves unknown-class detection without sacrificing unseen-class accuracy: on Table 4 it raises AUROC over the best competing baseline by 5.22% (CUB), 14.12% (AWA2), and 4.64% (FLO), is best on SUN by a smaller margin, and gives the best FPR95 on all four datasets (e.g., 12.42% FPR95 reduction on AWA2). Ablation (Table 5) shows the full L_ase + phi_open beats simpler unknown-generation strategies (Mixup, Uniform Noise, Semantic+Noise, Adversarial Features) in AUROC on all four datasets, and the end-to-end phi_open open score beats post-hoc ODIN/MSP scores on CUB/AWA2/FLO. ASE also generalizes: it leads under generalized ZS-OSR (Table 6) and is dramatically strong under a ZS-OOD setting (Table 7), reaching AUROC 97.2/99.0/99.9 with FPR95 4.0/5.3/0.3 on CUB-AWA2/CUB-FLO/CUB-SUN.
core_contribution: The paper defines and studies 'Zero-Shot Open-Set Recognition' (ZS-OSR): a model is trained under the zero-shot learning (ZSL) setting (images of seen classes + class-level semantic embeddings of seen and unseen classes) and must, at test time, correctly classify unseen-class images while rejecting images of 'unknown' classes for which neither samples nor semantic information were available in training. The authors' OWN contributions are threefold: (1) they formalize ZS-OSR (distinguishing it from ZSL and from OSR, per their Table 1) and build the first benchmark by adapting four standard ZSL datasets (CUB, AWA2, FLO, SUN) into ZS-OSR splits, plus a set of combined ZSL+OSR baselines; (2) they propose ASE (Adversarial Semantic Embeddings), a generative method that learns semantic embeddings of unknown classes which are tightly distributed around but separable from the unseen-class embeddings, then uses an off-the-shelf generative ZSL model to synthesize unknown-class features and trains a K+1 'unknowns-informed' open-set classifier; (3) they show ASE outperforms the combined baselines and extends to generalized ZS-OSR and ZS-OOD settings. Note: OSR methods that generate adversarial samples to represent unknowns (refs [13,14]) are CITED prior work, not this paper's contribution — the paper distinguishes ASE by operating in both semantic and feature spaces rather than the feature space alone.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a computer-vision recognition paper, not a security paper. The term 'adversarial' denotes an adversarial (GAN-style / energy-based) learning objective used to push generated unknown-class feature prototypes away from unseen-class features — it does NOT refer to adversarial attacks, evasion, poisoning, or robustness against a malicious actor. There is no attacker, no threat surface, and no security claim in the paper. The cluster label 'Adversarial Robustness' appears to be a misclassification driven by the word 'adversarial'. No threat model is invented here.
limitations: Results are reported by the authors on their own newly-constructed splits, so external comparability is limited (they introduce the task, so no independent prior numbers exist). ASE depends on the quality of an off-the-shelf generative ZSL backbone (TF-VAEGAN); the paper itself notes closed-set generation biases can degrade quality. Gains are uneven — on SUN the AUROC improvement over baselines is marginal (72.69 vs. e.g. 71.63 for MSP) and ASE's Acc (73.61) and FPR95 (70.41) are only comparable, not clearly best; ASE-enabled MSP even wins AUROC on SUN. Openness-robustness study is limited to CUB. The unknown/unseen class splits are chosen by randomly halving unseen classes, which may not reflect harder real-world distribution shifts (partly addressed by the separate ZS-OOD experiment). No security or robustness-under-attack evaluation is present (nor claimed).

datasets_used: CUB (Caltech-UCSD-Birds 200-2011): 11,788 images, 312-dim attributes, 150 seen / 25 unseen / 25 unknown classes
AWA2 (Animals with Attributes 2): 37,322 images, 85-dim attributes, 40 seen / 5 unseen / 5 unknown classes
FLO (Oxford Flowers): 8,189 images, 1,024-dim embeddings, 82 seen / 10 unseen / 10 unknown classes
SUN: 14,340 images, 102-dim attributes, 645 seen / 36 unseen / 36 unknown classes
ZS-OOD test sets built from CUB unseen classes combined with 25 classes each from AWA2, FLO, SUN (CUB-AWA2, CUB-FLO, CUB-SUN)
quantitative_results: AUROC gain over best baseline: +5.22% (CUB), +14.12% (AWA2), +4.64% (FLO); FPR95 reduced by 12.42% on AWA2
Ablation (Table 5) full L_ase+phi_open AUROC: CUB 80.20, AWA2 81.99, FLO 72.78, SUN 72.69, vs. Adversarial Features 74.92/54.70/73.19/70.75 and Semantic+Noise 63.74/69.02/63.22/54.77
Generalized ZS-OSR (Table 6) ASE FPR95/AUROC: CUB 77.8/75.9, AWA2 73.8/78.7, FLO 69.4/79.1, SUN 87.5/61.8 (best AUROC on all four datasets)
ZS-OOD (Table 7) ASE FPR95/AUROC: CUB-AWA2 4.0/97.2, CUB-FLO 5.3/99.0, CUB-SUN 0.3/99.9
Config: 50 unknown-class embeddings per unseen class, 1,000 synthetic samples per embedding, ResNet101 features, results averaged over 5 runs
baselines_compared: APN [15] (combined with MSP; non-generative ZSL baseline)
GCM-CF [38] with MSP (generalized ZS-OSR setting only)
All generative baselines use TF-VAEGAN [6] as the ZSL backbone, same as ASE
